1083	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1084	
1085	**DEFAULT: describe verbally; write code only if asked.**
1086	
1087	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the exact race:** "The race is in `boost_term`: `node.frequency += weight` is a read-modify-write. Two threads both read 7, both write 8 — one boost is lost. Separately, a reader walking the trie while a writer is creating a brand-new term's nodes could see a half-linked path."
1088	
1089	🗣️ **Step 2 — State the solution:** "Because the workload is read-heavy — tons of `complete` calls, few boosts — I use a READ-WRITE lock, not a coarse mutex. Readers take the shared side and run in parallel; a writer takes the exclusive side, so no reader and no other writer is inside while it mutates. That makes the increment atomic and guarantees readers never see a partial trie."
1090	
1091	🗣️ **Step 3 — Trade-off:** "A read-write lock costs more than a plain `Lock` and needs writer-preference to avoid starving boosts under heavy reads — which I built in. If writes ever became frequent, a plain mutex (or per-subtree locks) might be simpler, but here reads dominate so the read-write lock is the right call."
1092	
1093	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed (if asked):** "Across servers an in-process lock protects only one process. I'd shard the trie by first character or first 2 chars across nodes (prefix routing), and within a shard push the increment to a store with an atomic counter — Redis `INCR` or a DB `UPDATE … SET freq = freq + ? WHERE term = ?` — so the add is atomic without a held lock. Reads hit a per-shard cached top-K."
1094	
1095	**CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY**
1096	
1097	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1098	|---|---|---|
1099	| **Race condition** | Two threads touch shared state at once, result depends on timing | `node.frequency += weight` in `boost_term` |
1100	| **Lost update** | Two read-modify-writes overlap, one increment vanishes | the boost race the write lock prevents |
1101	| **Critical section** | The block that must run as one atomic step | the walk-create + increment in `boost_term` |
1102	| **Read-write (shared/exclusive) lock** | Many readers share; a writer is exclusive | `_ReadWriteLock` in the thread-safe subclass |
1103	| **Reader / writer preference** | Who jumps the queue when both wait | writer-preferring — queued boosts block new readers |
1104	| **Writer starvation** | A writer never gets in because readers keep arriving | prevented by `_writers_waiting` gating new readers |
1105	| **Atomic** | All-or-nothing, no half-done state observable | what the write lock gives the increment |
1106	| **Lock granularity** | How much one lock covers | one structure-wide read-write lock (the trie is one shared object) |
1107	| **Pessimistic locking** | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process read-write lock |
1108	| **Optimistic / CAS** | Don't lock; write conditionally and retry | the distributed `UPDATE … WHERE` / Redis `INCR` |
1109	| **Deadlock** | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: only one lock, never nested across instances |
1110	
1111	> 🎙️ "This is a lost-update race in the critical section. Because reads dominate I take a pessimistic, writer-preferring read-write lock — shared for queries, exclusive for boosts — and for the distributed case I switch to an atomic compare-and-set / `INCR` so I don't hold a lock across servers."
1112	
1113	**CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A**
1114	
1115	> ❓ "Why a read-write lock and not a plain `Lock`?"
1116	> "Because the workload is overwhelmingly reads — typeahead fires `complete` on every keystroke, boosts are rare. A plain mutex would serialize all those reads against each other for no reason; a read-write lock lets unlimited readers share and only blocks them when a boost needs exclusive access. That's a throughput win exactly proportional to the read/write skew."
1117	
1118	> ❓ "Doesn't a read-write lock risk starving writes?"
1119	> "Yes with a naive implementation — if readers keep arriving the reader count never drains. I built it writer-preferring: `acquire_write` bumps `_writers_waiting` first, and `acquire_read` waits while any writer is queued. So a boost is guaranteed to proceed once current readers finish; it can't be starved."
1120	
1121	> ❓ "Why not lock per trie node so different prefixes don't block?"
1122	> "Per-node locking sounds finer but a single boost walks and may create several nodes — you'd need to lock a whole path in order, which invites deadlock and lots of lock churn for microsecond critical sections. One structure-wide read-write lock keeps it simple and correct; reads already run fully in parallel, which is where the contention actually is."
1123	
1124	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1125	> "None internally — there's exactly one lock and it's never acquired nested in a different order. The read and write hooks each acquire-then-release in a `finally`, so even an exception while collecting still releases the lock. No lock-ordering cycle, no deadlock."
1126	
1127	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1128	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is a few microseconds and contention at the lock is low, so a short-held lock is simpler than a retry loop. For the DISTRIBUTED case I flip to optimistic — an atomic `INCR` or `UPDATE … WHERE`, because you can't hold a Python lock across servers and a conditional atomic write is the idiomatic primitive there."
1129	
1130	❓ **IF ASKED — the thread-safe version. Two parts.**
1131	
1132	**Part A — the no-op hooks + the subclass:**
1133	```python copy
1134	# ── §6 core: hooks do nothing ──────────────────────────────────────────────
1135	@contextmanager
1136	def _read_lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
1137	    yield
1138	
1139	@contextmanager
1140	def _write_lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
1141	    yield
1142	
1143	# ── §9 thread-safe subclass: override ONLY the two hooks ────────────────────
1144	class ThreadSafeAutocompleteService(AutocompleteService):
1145	    def __init__(self, ranking: RankingStrategy | None = None) -> None:
1146	        super().__init__(ranking)
1147	        self._rw = _ReadWriteLock()
1148

At minimum, add a test that exercises the corrupt-read path with brand-NEW terms created concurrently (current readers only ever query 3 pre-indexed terms, so the 'no partial trie' claim is barely exercised — no new nodes are created on the read prefix during the race).

findings.problem: The prose arrow notation contradicts the actual mermaid diagram. Diagram (line 357) correctly has `RankingStrategy <|.. FrequencyRankingStrategy` and (line 360) `AutocompleteService <|-- ThreadSafeAutocompleteService`. The walk writes them reversed as `FrequencyRankingStrategy ..|> RankingStrategy` (line 367) and `ThreadSafeAutocompleteService <|-- AutocompleteService` (line 369). The latter literally reads as 'AutocompleteService is a subclass of ThreadSafeAutocompleteService' — backwards. The rendered diagram is correct; only the explanatory text is inconsistent.

Review complete. Verdict: **FIX-FIRST**.

Both `main.py` (7 cases) and pytest (29 tests) pass, and the read-write lock is genuinely correct (writer-preferring, `notify_all` so no lost-wakeup, `finally`-release so no lock leak, no deadlock, ranking done outside the lock on immutable snapshots — I verified 20 concurrent shared readers + exclusive writers, and that the lock prevents lost updates 5/5 when the read-modify-write window is real). Patterns (Strategy, Template Method via lock hooks, Information Expert) are all genuinely realized. §3.5 API table, §4.1 class blocks, §6 code-vs-disk, and the 2 mermaid blocks (no semicolons in the sequenceDiagram) all check out.

Two MAJOR defects block ship:

1. **Tie-break ranking is wrong for prefix-pair ties** (strategies.py:39-44). The `_inverted` key negates each code point into a tuple, so when one tied term is a prefix of another the longer tuple wins the max-heap — `uber`/`uberx` at equal frequency return `["uberx","uber"]` instead of the contractually-required alphabetical `["uber","uberx"]`. This is a real correctness bug on the problem's own canonical example. Existing tests miss it because every tie they test is between equal-length, non-prefix terms.

2. **Concurrency test doesn't prove the lock** (tests/test_autocomplete.py:176-241). Good structure (Barrier + strict post-state), but `node.frequency += weight` is effectively GIL-atomic on CPython, so the lock-free base class passes the identical conservation assertion — I measured 0/20 lost updates without any lock even with the switch interval amplified to 1µs. The headline "no lost update" invariant is therefore trivially passable under the demo runtime. The "no corrupt/partial trie" claim is also barely exercised since readers only ever query 3 pre-indexed terms (no new nodes created on the read path during the race).

Plus two MINORs: a mermaid relationship-walk prose inconsistency (arrows reversed vs the correct diagram, _response.md:367/369) and an overly broad `pytest.raises(Exception)` (test_autocomplete.py:71).
